meta.title: scribe

source.publication: Section 4: Roots, Prefixes, Suffixes and Foreign Words (owner-supplied reference)
source.usage_note: Personal study only. Root, words and meanings transcribed faithfully from the source PDF; a small number of well-attested real words on the same root are included where the source list was too short to form a complete family (4 to 8 members). Parts, glosses, context sentences, mentor note, attempt and construct options are the teaching layer, authored for the Language Garden (LANGUAGE_GARDEN_BIBLE §10).

meta.reviewer_notes: Batch 2: transcribed from the owner's roots (Greek and Latin) reference; 3 taught, 2 reach.

root.label: scribe
root.mentor_note: scribe is one of the busiest roots in English, and it always keeps its literal sense close by.

attempt.direction_prompt: A doctor listening, or one ordering medicine?

context_sentences: The doctor examined the results once more before deciding what to prescribe.
Her physician refused to prescribe anything stronger until the milder option had been tried for a month.
The witness was asked to describe exactly what she had seen, in the order she had seen it.
No brochure could really describe how cold the wind gets on that ridge at dawn.
The jeweller offered to inscribe the ring with a short line chosen by the couple.
Names of the fallen were inscribed along the entire length of the memorial wall.
The council moved to proscribe the sale of fireworks anywhere inside the town limits.
Certain books were proscribed by the regime for decades before the ban was finally lifted.

construct_options.text: To forbid or officially prohibit something
To recommend a medical treatment for an illness
To publicly praise and formally approve of something

context_sentences: He scribbled the address on a napkin, already halfway out the door.
The margins of her old textbooks were scribbled with notes only she could still decipher.

construct_options.text: To write quickly and carelessly, often illegibly
To carve a permanent inscription into stone
To formally forbid something in writing
